perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees not to use the other's Confidential Information for any purpose other than for the specific consultation regarding the subject matter of this Agreement. Any other use of such Confidential Information shall be made only upon the prior written consent from an authorized representative of the party which disclosed such information (hereinafter the "Disclosing Party") or pursuant to subsequent agreement between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party may consider using the other's Confidential Information for purposes related to the consultation regarding the subject matter of this Agreement. Any other use of such Confidential Information may be considered upon receiving consent from a representative of the Disclosing Party or via an agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees not to use' to 'may consider using' and 'shall be made only upon the prior written consent' to 'may be considered upon receiving consent' introduces significant ambiguity. It weakens the restriction on using confidential information without clear authorization, making it discretionary rather than mandatory.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). CUTSA aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. By making the use of confidential information more discretionary, the agreement becomes less aligned with the protective measures intended by CUTSA, especially if the 'consideration' and 'agreement' are not properly defined or enforced, which allows for potential misappropriation without clear legal recourse.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates adherence to confidentiality through the phrase 'agrees not to use,' while the modified text uses 'may consider using,' which introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation. The original requires 'prior written consent,' but the modified version states actions 'may be considered upon receiving consent.' This directly undermines the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), as the Law Explanation states. Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' as 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent,' weakening the explicit requirement of consent goes against the protections intended by CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall use the same degree of care to avoid publication or dissemination of the Confidential Information as it would with respect to its own confidential information. These efforts shall specifically include document control measures, such as numbered copies and sign out logs, and imposing on all employees, agents and other representatives of Receiving Party restrictions at least as strict as required by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party should endeavor to use a reasonable degree of care to avoid publication or dissemination of the Confidential Information. These efforts might include document control measures and imposing restrictions on employees.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates a specific standard of care and explicit document control measures. The modified text replaces 'shall use' with 'should endeavor to use', weakens the standard of care to 'reasonable', and replaces 'shall specifically include' with 'might include', making the document control measures optional. The phrase "at least as strict as required by this Agreement" has been removed to further weaken the restriction.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code section 1708 states that every person is bound to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his rights. By weakening the requirements to protect the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information, the Receiving Party is no longer being held to as high of a standard of care, meaning they are now more likely to injure the property or infringe upon the rights of the Disclosing Party. If the Receiving Party were to publish or disseminate the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information as a result of the lowered standard of care, this would be a violation of California Civil Code section 1708.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1708. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1708. Every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his or her rights. (Amended by Stats. 2002, Ch. 664, Sec. 38.5. Effective January 1, 2003.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Civil Code section 1708. The original contract mandates a specific standard of care ('shall use') and explicit document control measures ('shall specifically include'). The modified text weakens this standard by using 'should endeavor to use' and 'might include,' making the document control measures optional. As stated in the scraped law snippet, 'Every person is bound, without contract, to abstain from injuring the person or property of another, or infringing upon any of his or her rights.' By weakening the original contract's mandatory requirements for protecting confidential information, the changed text makes it more likely that the Receiving Party could injure the property or infringe upon the rights of the Disclosing Party.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be generally interpreted in accordance with legal principles.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'the laws of the State of California' with 'generally interpreted in accordance with legal principles' removes the specified jurisdiction and introduces ambiguity. It's unclear what 'legal principles' are being referred to, making the governing law uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1858 states that in the construction of a statute or instrument, the office of the judge is simply to ascertain and declare what is in terms or in substance contained therein, not to insert what has been omitted, or to omit what has been inserted; and where there are several provisions or particulars, such a construction is, if possible, to be adopted as will give effect to all. This is also true of contractual interpretation. Because the location is unspecified, California statutory and case law regarding contractual interpretation is inapplicable. Without a jurisdiction, the judge is now forced to insert what has been omitted.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1858. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1858. In the construction of a statute or instrument, the office of the Judge is simply to ascertain and declare what is in terms or in substance contained therein, not to insert what has been omitted, or to omit what has been inserted; and where there are several provisions or particulars, such a construction is, if possible, to be adopted as will give effect to all. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies California law, while the changed text refers to 'generally interpreted in accordance with legal principles,' creating ambiguity by not specifying the governing jurisdiction. CCP 1858 states the judge's office is to declare what is in the statute or instrument and not insert what has been omitted. Here, without a specified jurisdiction, the judge is forced to insert what has been omitted. This alteration contradicts the law by removing a defined legal standard and requiring a court to insert missing terms, falling under Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation.
